title: Cache-Aside Pattern (Redis Implementation)
description: Cache-aside using Redis as the caching layer; configure TTL, eviction policy (allkeys-lru recommended), and connection pooling. Adds Redis-specific operational concerns: cluster mode, persistence settings, and AUTH configuration.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min represents a small-scale deployment using Redis cache.t3.micro instance (~$15/month on AWS ElastiCache, similar on Azure/GCP) suitable for development or low-traffic applications with basic caching needs. Max represents a production-grade high-availability Redis cluster with multiple cache.r6g.xlarge nodes (~$300-500 each), data persistence, multi-AZ replication, and cross-region failover for enterprise workloads handling millions of cache operations per second. Mid-range ($200-500) covers typical production scenarios with cache.r6g.large instances and standard replication.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity implementation. Requires understanding of cache invalidation strategies, TTL management, read-through/write-through patterns, and handling cache misses. Development effort includes: implementing cache key design and namespacing (2-4 hours), coding cache-aside logic with proper error handling and fallback mechanisms (8-12 hours), configuring Redis connection pooling and timeout settings (2-4 hours), implementing cache warming strategies (4-6 hours), and thorough testing of cache invalidation scenarios and race conditions (8-10 hours). Total estimate: 24-36 hours at $25-35/hour blended rate with AI assistance reducing complexity by 30-40%. Without AI assistance, this would typically cost $1,500-2,000.
provenance.licenseCost: Redis is open-source software under BSD 3-Clause license, completely free for commercial use. All major cloud providers (AWS ElastiCache, Azure Cache for Redis, Google Cloud Memorystore) offer managed Redis services with pay-as-you-go pricing included in monthly operational costs. Optional commercial Redis Enterprise with advanced features (active-active geo-distribution, enhanced security) would add $5,000-50,000+ annually, but is not required for standard cache-aside pattern implementation.

provides.reasoning: The cache-aside pattern fundamentally provides a caching layer that sits between the application and the data source, offering fast data access for frequently requested items.

This pattern provides a specific caching strategy (lazy loading) where the application is responsible for loading data into the cache on cache misses, distinguishing it from other caching patterns like write-through or read-through.
Redis provides distributed caching capabilities, allowing multiple application instances to share the same cache and scale horizontally.
Redis is an in-memory data store that provides extremely fast data access by keeping cached data in RAM rather than on disk.
Redis serves as the concrete cache-store implementation, satisfying the abstract cache-store role required by cache-aside, write-back-cache, write-through-cache, and caching-required-* patterns.

requires.reasoning: Redis is fundamentally a key-value store, and the cache-aside pattern requires this capability to function with fast key-based lookups for storing and retrieving cached data.
Cache-aside pattern requires a primary data source (database, API, etc.) to fetch data from on cache misses. Without a backing data source, the pattern cannot function.

warn_nfr.reason: Cache-aside--redis uses eventual consistency; read-your-writes requires synchronous cache invalidation which adds latency and complexity.
warn_nfr.message: cache-aside--redis: spec requires needsReadYourWrites=true but cache-aside is eventually consistent. Ensure your invalidation strategy is synchronous or use a read-through cache instead.

warn_nfr.reason: Cache-aside--redis adds infrastructure complexity (cache store, invalidation logic, serialization). At <10 read req/s the overhead rarely justifies the benefit.
warn_nfr.message: cache-aside--redis: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<10 req/s). Caching overhead (infrastructure, invalidation, serialization) may outweigh benefit at this scale. Consider simplifying to direct DB access.

supports_nfr.reason: Redis cache-aside pattern supports up to 99.99% availability with Redis Cluster for read operations.
Redis cache-aside achieves sub-millisecond cache hits but cache misses require database access adding latency.
P99 latency accounts for cache misses requiring database access and occasional Redis cluster failover.
Redis can handle up to 100K ops/sec per node, ceiling at 360M requests per hour with cluster configuration.
Cache-aside pattern supports all tenant isolation strategies through appropriate cache key prefixing.
Cache-aside pattern works regardless of PII presence, though PII requires additional encryption and TTL policies.
Cache-aside is GDPR-compatible with proper TTL policies and data deletion workflows for right-to-be-forgotten.
Cache-aside supports HIPAA compliance when combined with encryption-at-rest and appropriate access controls.
Cache-aside is CCPA-compatible with proper cache invalidation mechanisms for data deletion requests.
Cache-aside supports SOX compliance as it maintains database as source of truth with cache as read optimization.
Pattern ensures strict durability as all writes go to database first with cache being ephemeral layer.

supports_constraints.reason: Redis cache-aside pattern is cloud-agnostic and works on all major cloud providers and on-premises.
Cache-aside is language-agnostic with Redis client libraries available for all major programming languages.
Cache-aside pattern with Redis applies across all platform types from web applications to batch processing.
Cache-aside pattern explicitly requires caching feature to be enabled as it is fundamentally a caching strategy.
Pattern requires a key-value store for Redis to work.
Cache-aside can cache AI inference results or model metadata but does not require AI inference capability.
Cache-aside supports multi-tenancy through tenant-specific cache key prefixes and namespace isolation.
Cache-aside pattern works with or without batch processing, providing read acceleration for batch jobs.
Cache-aside is compatible with distributed transactions though cache invalidation adds complexity.
Cache-aside can cache streaming aggregates or reference data but does not require real-time streaming.
Cache-aside can cache vector search results but the pattern itself does not require vector search capability.
Cache-aside works with document stores as the backing database or with other data storage types.
Cache-aside can cache graph query results but does not require graph database as backing store.
Cache-aside can cache time-series aggregates or recent data points but works with any database type.
Cache-aside is particularly beneficial for OLTP workloads to reduce database load but not strictly required.
Cache-aside can cache OLAP query results to improve analytical query performance but works without OLAP.

eviction_policy.description: Redis eviction policy when max memory is reached. allkeys-lru removes least recently used keys, allkeys-lfu removes least frequently used, volatile-* only evicts keys with TTL, noeviction returns errors when full.

persistence.description: Data persistence strategy. none=no persistence (fastest, cache-only), rdb=periodic snapshots (good balance), aof=append-only file (most durable), both=maximum durability with performance cost.

max_memory.description: Maximum memory Redis can use. Larger values allow more cached data but increase costs. Choose based on working set size and budget.

ttl_strategy.description: Time-to-live strategy for cached items. fixed=expire after set time, sliding=reset TTL on access (keeps hot data), none=manual invalidation only.

clustering_enabled.description: Enable Redis clustering for horizontal scaling and high availability. Required for datasets larger than single-node memory or high-throughput scenarios. Adds complexity and cost.
